quote: I'm 74. I'm retired, and I'm on a limited income.
stewart_read: Doug volunteers age, retirement status, and financial constraint in the first 30 seconds — unprompted. This is simultaneously a bill-anchor opportunity and a vulnerability disclosure. Carter's response ('Are your bills pretty high, your electric bills?') skips the human moment entirely and jumps to data collection. The codex is explicit: vulnerability disclosure is a trust gift, not an interruption to procedure. The correct move is acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value. Carter did neither.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is moving quickly to qualify before Doug disengages. Rejected — Doug is warm and cooperative; there is no urgency signal that justifies skipping the acknowledgment.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure section. The coaching question for the 1-on-1: 'What would you have said if a neighbor told you that on their porch?' The answer to that question is the move Carter should have made here. Also flag the missed flip: Doug's limited income is the exact setup for the objection_inversion move ('What you can't afford is your current bill') — Carter had the material and didn't use it.

quote: We are both home now being retired, so we are using more energy than we did when we worked.
stewart_read: Doug is describing a structural load increase — two retired adults home all day, plus a 1,500 sq ft shop and garage. This is a high-consumption profile that makes the solar case stronger, not weaker. Carter responds with 'Okay. Gotcha.' and moves to qualifiers. The flip opportunity here is distinct from the fixed-income disclosure: Doug has just explained WHY his bill is high and WHY it will stay high. That is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may be saving the bill anchor for after he sees the actual utility bill. Rejected — the flip does not require the bill number. The emotional alignment-against-utility can be built from Doug's own words right now.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the bill_anchoring capture-acknowledge-flip sequence using Doug's exact words. Suggested rephrase direction (not verbatim): acknowledge that being home full-time means the bill isn't going down on its own, then use that as the reason seeing the actual numbers matters. The goal is to make Doug feel the pain of the status quo before the bill is even collected.

quote: I have memory problems, so I don't pay a lot of bills.
stewart_read: This is the first explicit cognitive-decline disclosure. Carter's response ('You're good. Yeah. No worries.') is a social reassurance, not an acknowledgment. More critically, Carter does not register the structural implication: if Doug has memory problems and doesn't pay the bills, the callback plan that will be constructed in the next two minutes — which depends on Doug relaying a message to his wife — is already compromised. This is both an empathy miss and a planning failure.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may be trying to keep Doug comfortable and not dwell on a sensitive topic. Directionally correct instinct, but the execution stops at reassurance rather than bridging to a practical adjustment (e.g., getting the wife's number now rather than later).
coaching_implication: The coaching point here is not just empathy — it is operational. Ask Carter: 'After Doug said that, what was your plan for making sure the wife actually got the message?' If Carter doesn't have an answer, that's the gap. The fix is to get the wife's direct contact in the moment and offer to reach out directly, rather than routing through Doug.

quote: You could, email her at simplygottarelax@gmail.com.
stewart_read: The wife's email is the only confirmed direct contact path to the decision-maker on this call. Carter accepts it as a bill-collection address ('I'll shoot my...') but never uses it as an appointment-setting anchor. The email could have been the vehicle for a locked callback confirmation — 'I'll send her an email right now with a couple of times that work, and she can pick one' — which would have bypassed the Doug-relay dependency entirely. Instead it becomes a passive follow-up address.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may intend to use the email for bill collection first, then schedule. Plausible, but given Doug's disclosed memory issues, the email should have been used to lock the appointment simultaneously, not sequentially.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the soft_outcome_distinction directive. The question for the 1-on-1: 'You had her email. What stopped you from sending her a time right then?' The answer reveals whether this is a knowledge gap (Carter didn't know he could use email for scheduling) or a sequencing habit (bill first, schedule second). Either way, the fix is to treat the wife's email as an appointment anchor, not just a bill-collection path.

quote: I'm not sure what works for her if you wanna call her later, and I'll tell her that you'll call. Is that possible?
stewart_read: Doug is handing Carter a warm-transfer path to the decision-maker and offering to prime her for the call. This is a cooperative signal, not a deflection. Carter's response is to ask if the wife is busy tomorrow — directionally correct (trying to lock a time) but the execution fails: no time is confirmed, the relay mechanism depends on Doug's memory (already flagged as unreliable), and Carter never attempts to reach the wife directly in the moment. The codex's spouse_decision protocol calls for including the co-decision-maker in the appointment; the practical gap here is identical even if the wife isn't formally on title.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter is trying to respect Doug's offer and not go around him. Reasonable instinct, but the correct move is to use Doug's offer as the bridge to a direct contact attempt, not as a substitute for one.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through what a locked callback looks like vs a soft callback. The coaching question: 'If Doug forgets to tell her — which he told you twice might happen — what's your plan?' The answer should be: get the wife's number now (which Carter eventually does, but only after the plan is already set), confirm a specific time, and offer to send a confirmation to her email. Carter got the number but never locked the time.

quote: Yeah. That phone number that I gave you is her cell phone number. So you would probably — if I tell her that you're gonna call, she would probably answer it.
stewart_read: Doug's phrasing is a fragility signal: 'if I tell her' and 'she would probably answer.' Both are conditional. The entire callback outcome rests on two uncertain events: Doug remembering to relay the message, and the wife choosing to answer an unknown number. Carter does not address either condition — no locked time, no direct outreach to the wife, no confirmation text to the wife's email. The call ends on this footing.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may plan to call the wife immediately after the call ends, before Doug has a chance to forget. Possible, but not stated — and the wife has no context for the call if Doug hasn't briefed her yet.
coaching_implication: This is the moment where Carter should have said something like: 'Let me try her right now while you're on the line — that way she knows it's coming and we can pick a time together.' That move collapses the relay dependency entirely. Flag this for the 1-on-1 as the specific execution gap in the callback plan.

quote: So it's all timers thing coming in.
stewart_read: Doug's second cognitive-decline disclosure — this time naming it explicitly ('all timers,' likely Alzheimer's). Carter responds 'All good. All good.' and wraps the call. This is the second instance of the same vulnerability being disclosed and the second instance of Carter responding with social reassurance rather than acknowledgment or adjustment. The structural consequence is now fully visible: the callback plan depends on Doug's memory, and Doug has just told Carter his memory is failing.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may be trying to keep the close of the call warm and not dwell on a difficult topic. The instinct to not make Doug feel bad about his condition is correct; the failure is not addressing the practical implication.
coaching_implication: Pair this moment with the 01:48 disclosure in the 1-on-1. Ask Carter: 'He told you twice. What did you do differently the second time?' The answer is nothing — and that's the coaching point. The fix is not to dwell on the condition but to use it as the reason to take the relay burden off Doug: 'Let me make sure your wife gets a heads-up directly so you don't have to worry about it.'

quote: we have a 1,500 square foot house and a 1,500 square foot shop and garage.
stewart_read: Doug is describing a dual-structure property — a residential home plus a 1,500 sq ft commercial-adjacent shop and garage. This is a materially higher load profile than a standard residential customer and a stronger solar case. Carter does not acknowledge the shop/garage as a distinct load driver or ask whether both structures are on the same meter. The bill collection that follows treats this as a single-structure account.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may be deferring the multi-structure question to the closer. Plausible, but the shop/garage detail should at minimum be flagged in the handoff — it changes the system sizing conversation entirely.
coaching_implication: Flag the shop/garage detail for the handoff brief (which it now is). In the 1-on-1, ask Carter: 'When he mentioned the shop and garage, what did you think about the size of the system he'd need?' If Carter didn't register it as a load signal, that's the gap. The coaching point is to treat multi-structure disclosures as enthusiasm signals that strengthen the case, not as background noise.
